data.title: OpenAI surpasses 3M paying business subscribers
data.description:
• OpenAI, the generative artificial intelligence company backed by Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), said on a livestream on Wednesday that it has surpassed 3M paying business subscribers, up from 2M paying users in February.
  • The figure includes enterprises that use ChatGPT Enterprise, ChatGPT Team and ChatGPT Edu customers, OpenAI added.
  • During the livestream, the company also announced it has ChatGPT, OpenAI's generative AI chatbot, will be able to access “connectors,” including Google Drive, Dropbox and more, without leaving ChatGPT.
  • In April, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said that more than 800M people were using ChatGPT, a doubling that occurred in “just a few weeks.”
  • OpenAI is now the world's most valuable startup, worth $300B as of its last funding round. Its valuation has nearly doubled since October 2024.

data.title: Chagee rallies after Citi starts off coverage with a bullish view
data.description:
Chinese tea chain Chagee (NASDAQ:CHA) landed its second bull rating on Wednesday after the IPO quiet period on the stock expired.

Citi started off coverage with a Buy rating and price target of $43.70, representing upside potential of more than 30%. 

Analyst Xiaopo Wei said Chagee is a fast-growing premium tea play, noting the company's roughly 20% market share among China's premium freshly made tea brands. Wei expects Chagee (NASDAQ:CHA) to continue to grow rapidly abroad as it leverages its automation and standardization capabilities. 

"As the leader in the premium segment, Chagee well differentiates itself from most value players with its solid long-term product strategy (simple core menu), managed franchise model (with technology-powered Five Things Online), strong brand equity (via social-media marketing), and high efficiency (via automation and standardization)," highlighted Wei.

Morgan Stanley, which also has a bull rating on Chagee (CHA), weighed in on the company as well. "Our channel checks and our AlphaWise consumer survey suggest that Chagee's (CHA) store operations (payback period and margins) are still relatively healthy and at competitive levels compared to peers, and that it continues to hold strong brand preference among consumers," updated analyst Lillian Lou and team.

At the end of Q1, Chagee (CHA) operated a total of 6,681 teahouses in Greater China and overseas, representing 64% growth in the number of teahouses from March 31, 2024.

Chagee (CHA) is a competitor with Luckin Coffee (OTCPK:LKNCY) and Starbucks (SBUX) in China and recently opened its first U.S. location.

Shares of Chagee (CHA) moved 6.5% higher in afternoon trading to $32.40. The IPO was priced at $28 per share.

data.title: Inotiv discloses completion of SEC investigation
data.description:
Inotiv (NASDAQ:NOTV) said on Wednesday that the U.S. SEC's Division of Enforcement finished its investigation into the company related to importation of non-human primates from Asia.

The company had previously disclosed that on May 23, 2023, it received a voluntary request from the SEC for documents related to its involvement, alongside Envigo Global Services and Orient BioResource Center, in the importation of non-human primates from Asia.

Post that, the SEC launched an investigation into the matter, which the company disclosed is now complete.

Based on the information received by the company, the SEC does not plan to suggest any enforcement action against Inotiv.

data.title: Mexico regulator expected to approve US Steel-Nippon tie-up - Reuters
data.description:
Mexico's antitrust regulator is expected to renew its approval of Nippon Steel's (OTCPK:NISTF) (OTCPK:NPSCY) bid for US Steel (NYSE:X) as soon as tomorrow, removing one of the last remaining hurdles to the tie-up, Reuters reported Wednesday.

The Cofece regulator previously approved the merger, but Mexican approval of the proposed acquisition, first announced in December 2023, has since expired and must be renewed for the merger to move forward.

US Steel (NYSE:X) shares are little changed after President Trump signed his order to double tariffs on steel and aluminum imports to 50%, as expected.

data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs scraps $500M hydrogen-based Ohio steel project
data.description:
Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF) said Tuesday it has officially abandoned its $500M hydrogen-based steel project in Middletown, Ohio, due to concerns over insufficient clean hydrogen supply, shifting focus to extend the life of its coal-fired blast furnace instead.

CEO Lourenco Goncalves cited delays in hydrogen production and pending Trump administration policies as key reasons for the decision, despite earlier support from a $500M U.S. Department of Energy grant under former President Biden that would have replaced the coal-based blast furnace with a hydrogen-fueled plant.

Goncalves said that even with the grant, Cleveland-Cliffs (NYSE:CLF) would need to spend an additional $1.1B, bringing the total project cost to $1.6B, but the company is in discussions with the Trump administration to renegotiate the grant to align with new energy priorities.

"Without hydrogen, the entire thing falls apart," Goncalves said at an event hosted by the American Iron and Steel Institute. "At the very least, I will not have hydrogen at the time I need for that specific project."

The CEO also said the U.S. Federal Reserve is holding down demand for steel used in construction and manufacturing by not cutting interest rates.

"The Fed is ridiculously late in acting," Goncalves said. "For the steel industry, it’s killing steel consumption."

data.title: Cogent reports insider transactions worth over $3M
data.description:
Cogent Communications (NASDAQ:CCOI) said in an SEC filing that CEO Dave Schaeffer sold stake worth more than $3M in the internet access and private network services provider.

Schaeffer sold 65K shares of common stock in two tranches at $46.3827 per share and $46.1337 per share.

data.title: Dollar Tree updates full-year EPS outlook to $5.15–$5.65 as it advances multi-price strategy and Family Dollar sale
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Dollar Tree (DLTR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Michael C. Creedon, CEO, began by stating that "In Q1, we delivered upside across every key metric and results exceeded the outlook we provided." Creedon emphasized the impact of the expanded assortment strategy in driving incremental traffic, ticket, and comps. Dollar Tree marked the opening of its 9,000th store and noted ongoing strength in the former 99 Cents Only portfolio.
  • Creedon highlighted that "Q1 comps and net sales both exceeded the high end of our outlook range, driven by a strong Valentine's Day and Easter," and reported that adjusted EPS from continuing operations was $1.26, "reflecting strong sales and ongoing focus in expense management."
  • The company added 2.6 million new customers during the quarter, with measurable sales improvement across all income levels, especially among households with incomes over $100,000. Creedon said, "Our gains in dollar and unit market share accelerated in Q1...we gained twice as much unit share in Q1 as we did in Q4."
  • The CEO also updated on the Family Dollar sale, indicating regulatory approval has been received and the transaction remains on track for an early summer close. Creedon reiterated that "selling Family Dollar sharpens our operational focus, strengthens our balance sheet and is highly cash flow accretive."
  • Stewart F. Glendinning, CFO, stated, "First quarter adjusted EPS from continuing operations was $1.26, which exceeded the high end of our outlook range of $1.10 to $1.25." Glendinning reported an 11.3% revenue increase driven by 5.4% comparable store sales growth and a 7.4% increase in square footage.
  • Glendinning also addressed liquidity: "We ended the quarter with $1 billion in cash and cash equivalents," and noted that share repurchases exceeded $500 million year-to-date.

Outlook

  • The company is reiterating its full-year comp and revenue outlook, with net sales expected in the range of $18.5 billion to $19.1 billion and comparable same-store sales growth of 3% to 5%.
  • The full-year adjusted EPS from continuing operations is now expected to be $5.15 to $5.65, which Glendinning described as "essentially the same net income range implied by our previous outlook, adjusted to reflect our year-to-date share repurchases."
  • For Q2, Glendinning warned, "our Q2 adjusted EPS from continuing operations could be down as much as 45% to 50% year-over-year before reaccelerating in Q3 and Q4 to get us back on track to reach our full year target."
  • The company expects TSA-related income for fiscal 2025 to be approximately $85 million to $90 million, beginning in Q3 and Q4.
  • Capital expenditures are projected in the $1.2 billion to $1.3 billion range for the year, with 400 new store openings planned.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted operating income for the quarter was $388 million, a 1.4% increase from last year. Adjusted operating margin declined 80 basis points due to a 20 bp increase in gross margin, offset by a 100 bp increase in SG&A rate.
  • Adjusted net income was $270 million compared to $268 million in the prior year.
  • Inventory increased $247 million, or 10%, to $2.7 billion, with Glendinning noting, "part of the inventory increase also relates to the fact that we have 496 more stores this year than we had last year."
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $130 million, and the company ended the quarter with no borrowings under revolvers or commercial paper outstanding.

Q&A

  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Questioned the offset for $110 million in Q2 unanticipated costs and the potential for increased earnings volatility. Creedon responded, "we've really created a more nimble and flexible company...given the right amount of time, we're able to offset those pressures."
  • Edward Kelly, Wells Fargo: Asked about maintaining 35%-36% gross margin despite tariffs. Glendinning replied, "we believe that we are in a position this year to deliver that gross margin," citing strong assortment and merchant execution.
  • Paul Lejuez, Citi: Inquired about price points and higher-income customers. Creedon stated the focus is on customer needs, not a "break the dollar moment," and affirmed multi-price resonates with higher-income customers.
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: Sought clarity on AUR in 3.0 stores and TSA reimbursements. Creedon explained the $1.35 AUR is chain-wide, and Glendinning detailed that costs for TSA services performed will be reimbursed.
  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about the balance of traffic and ticket in comps. Creedon said, "We want to create a sticky relationship with that customer, such that we can count on them in the future."
  • Rupesh Parikh, Oppenheimer: Questioned guidance reiteration despite strong Q1 and Q2. Glendinning cited confidence in the range and flagged expected higher Q2 costs.
  • Chuck Grom, Gordon Haskett: Asked about performance of 1.0/2.0 vs. 3.0 stores and SG&A guidance. Creedon said all formats lifted, with 3.0 strongest; Glendinning highlighted increased investment in store payroll and depreciation.
  • Other analyst questions focused on tariff impacts, inventory management, multi-price format learnings, and advertising strategies.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on cost volatility, gross margin sustainability amid tariffs, and guidance conservatism, signaling a slightly skeptical to neutral tone—especially regarding the ability to offset near-term cost pressures and the rationale for not raising guidance after a strong quarter.
  • Management maintained a confident, yet cautious, tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing flexibility, a proven mitigation toolkit, and "confidence in our ability to navigate whatever near-term challenges we may face." Defensive phrases such as "given the right amount of time" and "we're more flexible today than we've ever been" were used.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained optimistic but acknowledged greater near-term volatility, while analysts’ questioning grew more pointed on cost headwinds and guidance assumptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full-year revenues and comps was reiterated, but EPS outlook was updated to reflect share repurchases.
  • The previous quarter detailed mitigation of the first round of tariffs; this quarter, management outlined a specific Q2 cost headwind of $110 million and warned of a 45%-50% Q2 EPS decline before recovery in the second half.
  • Strategic focus remains on multi-price expansion, but this quarter provided more detail on Family Dollar sale timing and expected cash position post-transaction.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on cost absorption, tariff timing, and SG&A increases, while management’s confidence in cost mitigation was accompanied by more explicit warnings of near-term volatility.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted tariff and cost inflation as significant challenges, noting, "the tariff landscape is highly fluid and changing week-to-week."
  • There is expected near-term profit pressure from timing mismatches between cost absorption and mitigation benefits, particularly in Q2.
  • Risks to inventory management and execution were flagged amid rapid store expansion and supply chain disruptions.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of margin performance and the impact of higher SG&A and labor costs.

Final Takeaway

Dollar Tree’s management emphasizes strong Q1 top-line momentum and customer growth across all income cohorts, powered by the expanded multi-price strategy and ongoing store conversions. Despite projecting significant short-term earnings headwinds due to tariffs and cost investments in Q2, the company reaffirms its full-year sales and EPS outlook, expressing confidence in its ability to deploy mitigation levers and recover profitability in the second half. The upcoming close of the Family Dollar sale is expected to further strengthen the balance sheet and operational focus, positioning Dollar Tree for long-term growth as a standalone business.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: JPMorgan said to accept crypto ETFs as loan collateral for trading, wealth clients
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) is planning to accept crypto-tied assets as collateral -- starting with crypto exchange-traded funds -- for loans to clients in its trading and wealth-management units, according to a media reported published Wednesday.

In the weeks ahead, the U.S.'s largest bank will start providing financing against BlackRock's (BLK) iShares Bitcoin Trust (NASDAQ:IBIT), Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The news comes amid a friendlier regulatory environment under pro-crypto U.S. President Donald Trump. That, in turn, is prompting growing institutional acceptance of the digital asset space.

For certain wealth clients, JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM) will also start factoring in their crypto assets into their total liquid net worth, treating such holdings on par with stocks, vehicles and art for loan eligibility.

JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon has long been a crypto skeptic, though he noted that the bank would still enable clients to purchase it. “I don’t think we should smoke, but I defend your right to smoke,” he had said at JPM's investor day last month, Bloomberg reported. “I defend your right to buy Bitcoin, go at it.”

Marko Kolanovic, the former chief market strategist and co-head of global research at J.P. Morgan, had strong words last month for those touting the global race for crypto supremacy as he continued to slam the asset class.

data.title: AI infrastructure continues to mount as Amazon announces $10B data center project
data.description:
The build out of artificial intelligence infrastructure continues, as Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) announced today it is investing $10B to expand its data center footprint in North Carolina.

"Amazon's $10 billion investment in North Carolina underscores our commitment to driving innovation and advancing the future of cloud computing and AI technologies," said David Zapolsky, Amazon's chief global affairs and legal officer. "This investment will position North Carolina as a hub for cutting-edge technology, create hundreds of high-skilled jobs, and drive significant economic growth."

The sizable investment is expected to create 500 high-skilled jobs in the Amazon Web Services data center supply chain. 

"Amazon's investment is among the largest in state history and will bring hundreds of good-paying jobs and an economic boost to Richmond County," said N.C. Gov. Josh Stein.

Richmond County is located between Charlotte and Fayetteville. 

As part of the investment, Amazon also vowed to work with North Carolina educational institutions to increase training programs and work-based learning opportunities in the data center industry.

The number of large data centers operated by hyperscale providers increased to 1,136 by the end of 2024, as the total has more than doubled since 2019, according to Synergy Research Group. The research firm expects the number to keep growing by 130 to 140 each year for the foreseeable future.

Amazon, Microsoft (MSFT) and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) are the leading cloud providers. The U.S. currently contains 54% of global data center capacity, according to Synergy data. China ranks second with 16%. Europe houses 15%.

"Historically, the average size of new data centers was increasing gradually, but this trend has become supercharged in the last few quarters as companies build out AI-oriented infrastructure," said John Dinsdale, a chief analyst at Synergy Research Group.

data.title: Apple launch of AI features in China held up trade war: report
data.description:
• Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) launch of its Apple Intelligence features in China is being held up by the trade war between China and the U.S., the Financial Times reported.
  • The respective applications of Apple and Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) — which is working with Apple on the Apple Intelligence launch — are being held up by the Cyberspace Administration of China, the news outlet added, citing two people familiar with the matter.
  • Last month, it was reported that the White House has scrutinized the deal between Apple and Alibaba after concerns were raised.
  • Apple and Alibaba did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • Alibaba Chairman Joseph Tsai said in February that iPhones would use Alibaba's AI technology to aid with Apple Intelligence. Apple Intelligence was first announced in June 2024. 
  • Apple is set to host its annual developers conference next week, with a keynote address slated for June 9 at 1 p.m. EST.
  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
